questions.question: Dunmore Ltd has been unable to pay its debts for six months. Its directors, wishing to avoid a compulsory winding up, resolve to place the company into creditors' voluntary liquidation. Under the Companies Act 2014, which section governs creditors' voluntary liquidation and what is the key procedural requirement distinguishing it from a members' voluntary liquidation?

options.A: Part 11, s.580 — the key distinction is that in a CVL the directors must execute a statutory declaration of solvency before the resolution is passed
options.B: Part 11, s.587 — the key distinction is that in a CVL no declaration of solvency is made and creditors play a central role, including their ability to nominate a liquidator who takes precedence over the members' nominee
options.C: Part 11, s.569 — the distinction is that a CVL requires a High Court petition whereas an MVL requires only a special resolution
options.D: Part 11, s.580 — the distinction is that a CVL requires a unanimous resolution while an MVL requires only a 75% special resolution

questions.explanation: Under the CA 2014, a Creditors' Voluntary Liquidation (CVL) is governed by Part 11 provisions including s.587. The defining characteristic is the absence of a solvency declaration — directors cannot honestly declare solvency. In a CVL: creditors hold a meeting concurrent with (or immediately after) the members' meeting; creditors may nominate their own choice of liquidator, and if different from the members' nominee, the creditors' nominee prevails (s.587(3)). By contrast, a Members' Voluntary Liquidation (MVL) under s.580 requires directors to make a statutory declaration of solvency (that the company will pay all debts within 12 months). s.569 governs compulsory winding up by court order.

questions.question: A creditor of Ashfield Ltd has obtained a judgment for €25,000 and served a formal demand for payment. The company has failed to pay within the statutory period. Under s.570 of the Companies Act 2014, which of the following most accurately states the grounds on which a company is deemed unable to pay its debts?

options.A: A company is deemed unable to pay its debts only if a court-appointed examiner certifies insolvency following an independent expert's report
options.B: A company is deemed unable to pay its debts if it fails to satisfy a written demand for a sum exceeding €10,000 within 21 days, or if execution is returned unsatisfied, or if it is proved to the court's satisfaction that the company cannot pay its debts (balance sheet or cash flow insolvency)
options.C: A company is deemed unable to pay its debts only on balance-sheet insolvency (liabilities exceeding assets), not cash-flow insolvency alone
options.D: A company is deemed unable to pay its debts upon failure to pay any creditor any amount within 30 days of the creditor's written demand, regardless of the sum

questions.explanation: Section 570 CA 2014 sets out the Irish law tests for inability to pay debts: (1) failure to satisfy a written demand for a sum exceeding €10,000 (the 'statutory demand') within 21 days; (2) unsatisfied execution (sheriff returns nulla bona); or (3) proof to the court's satisfaction that the company cannot pay its debts as they fall due (cash-flow insolvency), taking future contingent liabilities into account. This is a tripartite test — either limb suffices. The threshold is €10,000, not unlimited (option D wrong), and cash-flow insolvency is independently sufficient (option C wrong). No examiner certification is needed (option A wrong).

questions.question: In Re Burren Springs Ltd [2013] IEHC, Finlay Geoghegan J considered the basis on which the court should make a winding-up order where a company is insolvent and there is a clear case for winding up. Which principle did she apply?

options.A: The court has an absolute discretion to refuse a winding-up order even on proved insolvency if it considers examinership a better alternative
options.B: A winding-up order is granted ex debito justitiae (as of right) once a creditor establishes inability to pay debts — the court has no general discretion to refuse where insolvency is established and the petitioning creditor is a bona fide creditor
options.C: The court applies a balancing test weighing the interests of all stakeholders, including employees and the wider community, before granting a winding-up order
options.D: A winding-up order is available only as a last resort; the court must first explore restructuring alternatives including examinership before ordering liquidation

questions.explanation: Re Burren Springs [2013] confirmed the ex debito justitiae principle: where a creditor establishes that a company is unable to pay its debts and the creditor has a legitimate and subsisting debt, the court is entitled (and generally obliged) to make a winding-up order as of right — it is not purely at the court's general discretion. While the court retains some residual discretion (e.g., to adjourn where a rescue is genuinely possible, or to refuse if the petition is an abuse), the ex debito justitiae principle means that a proved insolvent company cannot simply invoke a discretion to avoid winding up. The court does not conduct a stakeholder balancing exercise (option C) or require examinership to be exhausted first (option D).

questions.question: Rockport Ltd is being wound up compulsorily. Its assets include a long-term lease over a Dublin warehouse that the liquidator considers onerous (below-market rent with significant ongoing maintenance obligations). Under s.561 of the Companies Act 2014, what power does the liquidator have, and what are the conditions for its exercise?

options.A: The liquidator may disclaim the lease with the consent of the High Court, provided the lease was entered into within 12 months of commencement of winding up
options.B: The liquidator may disclaim the lease as onerous property with leave of the court, within a reasonable time of becoming aware of the property; disclaimer releases the company from liability but does not affect third-party rights
options.C: The liquidator may assign the lease to any third party at below-market value without landlord consent, as the company's insolvency displaces the landlord's right of consent
options.D: The liquidator may disclaim the lease only if the landlord agrees to waive arrears of rent accrued prior to the commencement of winding up

questions.explanation: Section 561 CA 2014 empowers a liquidator in a compulsory winding up to disclaim onerous property (including unprofitable contracts, unsaleable land, and property that may give rise to a liability to pay money or perform an onerous act) with leave of the court. The disclaimer: (i) releases the company and its property from liability; (ii) must be exercised within a reasonable time of the liquidator becoming aware of the property; and (iii) does not affect the rights of third parties inter se (e.g., guarantors remain liable). Leave of court is required — it is not an automatic power. The landlord's consent is not required (option D wrong).

questions.question: A members' voluntary liquidation of Broadstone Ltd is underway. The liquidator subsequently discovers that the company's assets are insufficient to pay all creditors in full — the company was insolvent at the time the solvency declaration was made. What must the liquidator do?

options.A: Continue with the MVL but apply to the High Court for additional time to realise assets before determining whether a conversion is necessary
options.B: Immediately convene a creditors' meeting and convert the liquidation to a creditors' voluntary liquidation under s.581 CA 2014, at which point creditors gain the right to nominate a new liquidator
options.C: Apply to have the MVL converted to an examinership, since the company may still be rescued if the liquidator identifies a viable scheme of arrangement
options.D: Resign as liquidator and notify the CEA, who will then appoint an official liquidator to conduct the winding up on behalf of the State

questions.explanation: Where the liquidator in an MVL forms the opinion that the company will not be able to pay its debts in full within the period stated in the solvency declaration (12 months), s.581 CA 2014 requires the liquidator to: (i) immediately summon a meeting of creditors; (ii) lay a statement of assets and liabilities before that meeting; and (iii) the liquidation converts to a CVL. At the creditors' meeting, creditors may appoint their own nominated liquidator (who, if different from the existing liquidator, takes precedence under s.587(3)). The directors who made a false solvency declaration may face criminal liability under s.207 CA 2014.

questions.question: Under s.569 of the Companies Act 2014, the High Court may order the winding up of a company on a number of grounds. Which of the following is NOT a statutory ground for compulsory winding up under s.569?

options.A: The company has passed a special resolution that it be wound up by the court
options.B: The company is unable to pay its debts as defined by s.570
options.C: The court is of the opinion that it is just and equitable to wind up the company
options.D: The company has failed to hold its annual general meeting within the period prescribed by s.176

questions.explanation: Section 569 CA 2014 lists the grounds for compulsory winding up, which include: (a) special resolution to wind up by court; (b) failure to commence business within a year; (c) suspension of business for a year; (d) inability to pay debts (s.570); (e) just and equitable ground (s.569(1)(e)); and (f) oppression under s.212 where winding up is the appropriate remedy. Failure to hold an AGM is not a ground for compulsory winding up — the remedy for AGM default is a court order to convene a meeting (s.179 CA 2014) or a complaint to the CEA. Note also that LTDs are not required to hold AGMs under s.175, making option D particularly inappropriate.

questions.question: In a compulsory winding up of Glendale Ltd, the liquidator seeks to set aside a transaction where the company, six months before commencement of winding up, transferred its headquarters building to a connected company for no consideration. Under which provision of the Companies Act 2014 is such a transaction most directly challenged, and what must the liquidator prove?

options.A: s.604 (unfair preference) — prove that the company intended to prefer the connected company over other creditors at the time of the transfer
options.B: s.601 (fraudulent disposition) — prove that the transfer was made with intent to defraud creditors and occurred within the look-back period for connected parties
options.C: s.599 (related party transactions) — prove that proper board procedures for related party transactions were not followed
options.D: s.212 (oppression) — prove that the transfer constituted conduct oppressive to minority shareholders who were unaware of the transaction

questions.explanation: A gratuitous transfer to a connected party at an undervalue for no consideration is most directly addressed by s.601 CA 2014 (fraudulent disposition — formerly s.139 Companies Act 1990). The liquidator must prove: (i) a disposition of property; (ii) with intent to defraud creditors (actual fraudulent intent, not just an objective test); (iii) made at any time before the commencement of winding up. For connected parties, s.604 (unfair preference) may also be relevant, but the distinctive feature of a zero-consideration transfer is fraudulent intent under s.601. s.604 addresses preferences (paying one creditor over others), not gratuitous disposals. The court may set aside the transaction and order restoration of the property.

questions.question: Once a compulsory winding-up order is made by the High Court, what is the effect on legal proceedings against the company and what court approval is required to proceed?

options.A: All legal proceedings against the company are automatically stayed and cannot be resumed without leave of the court that made the winding-up order
options.B: Legal proceedings are automatically stayed only if the liquidator files a notice of stay with the High Court within 7 days of the winding-up order
options.C: All legal proceedings are stayed for 6 months only, after which they may proceed without court leave if the company's assets have not been distributed
options.D: Only secured creditors' enforcement proceedings are automatically stayed; unsecured creditors may continue legal proceedings to establish the quantum of their claims

questions.explanation: Under s.573 CA 2014, once a winding-up order is made by the court, no action or proceeding against the company can be commenced or continued except with leave of the court and subject to such terms as the court imposes. This automatic stay applies to all actions — secured and unsecured. The purpose is to prevent a race among creditors to obtain priority through litigation, and to centralise all claims through the liquidation process. The stay operates automatically upon the making of the order (option B wrong — no additional filing required). There is no 6-month limitation (option C wrong).

questions.question: In a liquidation, which of the following debts ranks as preferential under s.621 of the Companies Act 2014 and is payable ahead of floating charge holders?

options.A: Amounts owed to the company's solicitors for legal services rendered in the 12 months before winding up
options.B: Employees' wages and salary (up to a prescribed limit per employee) for services rendered in the four months before the commencement of winding up
options.C: All amounts owed to Revenue Commissioners for any tax liability arising in the three years before winding up
options.D: Loan repayments owed to the company's principal bank under a facility agreement signed two years before winding up

questions.explanation: Section 621 CA 2014 (reflecting the long-standing preferential creditor regime) provides that employees' wages and salaries up to a prescribed statutory maximum per employee, for services rendered in the four months immediately before the commencement of winding up, rank as preferential debts payable ahead of floating charge holders (but behind fixed charge holders and the costs of the liquidation). Revenue claims are preferential only for specific limited categories (PAYE/PRSI within time limits, not all tax debts). Solicitors' fees and bank loan repayments are ordinary unsecured debts ranking pari passu with general creditors (subject to any security held by the bank).

questions.question: Under s.631 of the Companies Act 2014, a liquidator has a general obligation to hold meetings of contributories and creditors. However, in a compulsory winding up, which body has primary supervisory responsibility for the liquidator's conduct?

options.A: The Corporate Enforcement Authority, which has statutory power to investigate and remove liquidators in all winding-up proceedings
options.B: The High Court, which retains ongoing supervisory jurisdiction over the conduct of compulsory winding-up proceedings, with the liquidator as an officer of the court
options.C: The committee of inspection (if appointed), which has exclusive supervisory power over the liquidator and whose decisions bind the court
options.D: The Revenue Commissioners, as the State's largest preferential creditor in most insolvencies, who exercise de facto supervisory control

questions.explanation: In a compulsory (court-ordered) winding up, the High Court retains ongoing supervisory jurisdiction as the winding up proceeds under its direction. The liquidator is an officer of the court and must act consistently with court orders and report to the court. Any creditor, contributory, or interested party may apply to the court to examine the liquidator's conduct (s.631). A committee of inspection may be appointed under s.632, but it is advisory/collaborative rather than having exclusive supervisory power binding the court (option C wrong). The CEA has powers to investigate but is not the primary supervisory body in an individual winding up (option A overstates its role).
